hints.Puer: 'Puer' is a noun meaning 'child/boy' — it is the subject
hints.nobis: 'nobis' is a pronoun in the dative case — it is not a verb
hints.natus: 'natus' alone is only the participle — together with 'est' it forms the perfect passive verb

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'natus est' translates to?

hints.to swim: 'to swim' is 'natare' (different word) — 'natus est' comes from 'nasci' (to be born)
hints.to know: 'to know' is 'scire' or 'noscere' — 'natus est' comes from 'nasci'

hints.Present: Present of 'nasci' would be 'nascitur' — 'natus est' uses the perfect participle + 'est'
hints.Imperfect: Imperfect of 'nasci' would be 'nascebatur' — 'natus est' is the perfect passive form
hints.Pluperfect: Pluperfect of 'nasci' would be 'natus erat' — 'natus est' uses 'est', not 'erat'

hints.patior, pati, passus sum, —: These are the principal parts of 'pati' (to suffer) — a deponent verb, but not this one
hints.morior, mori, mortuus sum, —: These are the principal parts of 'mori' (to die) — a deponent verb, but not this one
hints.sequor, sequi, secutus sum, —: These are the principal parts of 'sequi' (to follow) — a deponent verb, but not this one

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'puer' would be 'pueri' — 'Puer' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'puer' would be 'puerum' — 'Puer' is the nominative form
hints.Vocative: While 'puer' can be vocative, here it is the subject of 'natus est'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'pueri' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'pueri' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'pueri' ends in -i

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'nos' is 'nos' — 'nobis' is the dative/ablative form
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'nos' is 'nostri' or 'nostrum' — 'nobis' is dative/ablative
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'nos' is 'nos' — 'nobis' is the dative/ablative form

translation: A child is born to us
